introduction: Logical Deduction (Statements and Conclusions) asks whether a conclusion must follow from given statements. You decide what is definitely true, what is only possible, and what does not follow.
explanation:
CONCEPT

You are given one or more statements treated as true. Conclusions must be judged only on those statements—not on real-world knowledge. A conclusion follows only if it is forced by the statements.

HOW TO SOLVE

1) Accept statements as 100% true for the question.
2) For each conclusion, ask: Can I construct a case where statements hold but the conclusion fails? If yes, it does not follow.
3) "Some" does not mean "all"; "all" implies "some" in exam logic for positive universals (careful with exam convention).
4) Negative statements (no/none) restrict membership strongly.
5) For multiple conclusions, evaluate each independently, then pick the option describing which ones follow.

IMPORTANT RULES

• Do not use outside facts.
• Possibility ≠ certainty.
• From "All A are B" you may conclude "Some A are B" (standard exam convention).
• From "Some A are B" you cannot conclude "All A are B".
• From "No A are B" you can conclude "No B are A".

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Importing real-world truth.
2) Treating "some" as "most" or "all".
3) Assuming the converse of a universal affirmative (All A are B ⇏ All B are A).
4) Marking a conclusion that is only sometimes true.
5) Mixing up which conclusion follows when options list combinations.

formulas.title: Universal affirmative
formulas.content: All A are B → every A is inside B; converse not automatic

formulas.title: Particular
formulas.content: Some A are B → at least one A is B; not all

formulas.title: Universal negative
formulas.content: No A are B ↔ No B are A

formulas.title: Follows test
formulas.content: Conclusion follows iff it is true in every case allowed by the statements

shortcutTips.title: Venn sketch
shortcutTips.tip: Draw quick circles for All/Some/No to see forced overlap.

shortcutTips.title: Counterexample hunt
shortcutTips.tip: Try to break the conclusion while keeping statements true.

shortcutTips.title: Ignore real life
shortcutTips.tip: If statements say all cats are red, treat that as true here.

shortcutTips.title: Read option codes
shortcutTips.tip: For "only I", "only II", "either", "both", check each conclusion first, then match.

solvedExamples.problem: Statements: All pens are pencils. Some pencils are erasers. Conclusion: Some pens are erasers.
solvedExamples.solution: Does not follow — pens are inside pencils, but they may lie outside the eraser overlap.
steps: Some pencils overlap erasers; that overlap need not include pens.
Conclusion is possible but not necessary.

solvedExamples.problem: Statements: No car is a bike. All bikes are cycles. Conclusion: No car is a cycle.
solvedExamples.solution: Does not follow — cars are disjoint from bikes, but cycles may still include cars if cars are cycles that are not bikes.
steps: Cars and bikes do not overlap.
Cars could still be cycles; statement does not forbid it.

solvedExamples.problem: Statements: All mangoes are fruits. All fruits are food. Conclusions: (I) All mangoes are food. (II) Some food are mangoes.
steps: Mangoes ⊂ fruits ⊂ food → all mangoes are food.
Therefore some food (namely mangoes) are mangoes → II follows.
